data.title: 2 break out of French jail in ‘old-fashioned’ bed sheets escape
data.description:
Two prisoners escaped from a French jail using bed sheets after sawing through the bars of their cell, a prosecutor said on Thursday.
France has some of the worst prison overcrowding in Europe, and staff unions have complained that the state is neglecting normal jails as it moves narco criminals into new supermax prisons.
Guards noticed that the two men had escaped from the jail in the eastern city of Dijon shortly before dawn, the prisons authority said.
The pair “seem to have sawn through bars” and “fled using bed sheets,” Dijon prosecutor Olivier Caracotch said, without providing further details on how exactly they used the bedding.
The fugitives are a 19-year-old man held in pre-trial detention since October 2024 for attempted murder in a drug-related case, and a 32-year-old man incarcerated since 2023 over threats and violence against a partner, Caracotch said.

Union official Ahmed Saih, who represents prison officers at the jail, said the inmates used “old-fashioned, manual saw blades”.
“We’ve been warning about the risk of a jailbreak for months,” Saih said, noting earlier reports of saw blades found inside the prison.
He called for more staff and better equipment, including “gratings that cannot be sawn through”.
Dijon prison, built in 1853, is in poor condition, with 311 inmates for 180 places, according to the justice ministry.
“Prison is very hard here,” an inmate said on Thursday, after being released after eight months.
“There were three of us in a cell: two on bunk beds and one sleeping on the floor,” he said outside the prison gates.

Prisons are ‘suffering’
The prison break comes 10 days after another escape in the western city of Rennes.
A 37-year-old convict, who had more than a year still to serve for theft, fled during an outing with fellow prisoners to the city’s planetarium.
It was not immediately clear if he had been caught.
Justice Minister Gerald Darmanin sacked the prison’s director.
Three prison directors’ unions on Wednesday lashed out at the tough-talking right-wing minister, who is pushing through a plan to lock up the most dangerous drug traffickers in supermax prisons.
They accused him of “devoting all the resources of a debt-ridden state” to the high-security prisons for those accused of drug trafficking and jihadist attacks, and neglecting the “vast majority” of other jails.
“While the justice minister parades around in overfunded facilities, other [prison] services are suffering,” they said in a joint statement.

Darmanin last week announced the Dijon facility was scheduled to receive €6.3 million (US$7.3 million), as part of a programme to eradicate mobile phones from six French prisons.
France has some of the worst prison overcrowding in Europe, ranking third worst after Slovenia and Cyprus, according to a Council of Europe report published in July.
In early October, the national average was 135 inmates per 100 places available.
Notorious French drug baron Mohamed Amra, known as “The Fly”, was transferred to a new supermax prison in northern France in July.
Amra made international headlines when he escaped in May 2024 when the prison van he was being transported in was ambushed by gunmen and two prison guards were killed.
He was caught in Romania and extradited to France after a monthslong manhunt.

data.title: Hong Kong families endure unimaginable loss as fire death toll hits 75
data.description:
Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades has torn apart families as the death toll climbed to 75 on Thursday night, with many heavy-hearted relatives continuing to search for missing loved ones.
Firefighters searched late into the night, combing flats across seven blocks of Wang Fuk Court and finding more bodies.
Authorities were yet to release details about the deceased and injured as the blaze entered its second night.
In the evening, anxious families gathered at a temporary shelter in Tai Po and awaited news.

One woman, Winnie Hui, broke down upon hearing her baby had died in the fire.
“I am the mum who keeps posting non-stop on social media trying to find my baby,” she said. “I don’t know what to do. My baby was born after so much hardship.”
She earlier told the Post that her last phone call with her 68-year-old mother-in-law, Li Kin-yuk, was at 3.02pm on Wednesday, while the grandmother was caring for Hui’s six-month-old baby girl Ho Tsz-yan, at her flat in Wang Cheong House.
Li told Hui there was smoke outside and she would move to another flat on the same floor, but contact was lost soon after.
Hui’s father-in-law, 75, was also missing.

A day after Wednesday’s deadly fire engulfed seven of the eight buildings at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, the death toll rose to 75, with 76 injured and 279 reported missing.
At the Kwong Fuk Community Hall on Thursday afternoon, authorities displayed an album of charred body photos for families to identify loved ones.
Dozens queued orderly; some left sobbing uncontrollably after confirming identities.
“I can’t describe the feeling [when browsing the album]. There are so many [photos] of children,” said Cheung, who could not identify her missing sister and brother-in-law from the 23rd floor of Wang Cheong House.
She planned to return on Friday to check again.

Outside the hall, a 40-year-old Indonesian domestic helper named Yayuk wept while searching the album for her missing sister, Sri-wahyunt, carer to an 80-year-old in Wang Cheong House.
“My family says I am the only one who can find her, so I must find her,” she said.
A 70-year-old woman, Mrs Yeung, said her elder sister lived alone on the 16th floor of Wang Cheong House.
“I went to the temporary shelters and hospitals, but did not hear anything. So I prepared for the worst and came here, but I still have not found her,” she said, bursting into tears.
Many families issued appeals to find missing relatives.
A desperate mother who lives in Wang Tai House distributed photos of her five-year-old daughter Hannah Cheng and domestic helper Maryan, both missing after the fire.
On Instagram, one user appealed for his missing 16-year-old girlfriend, her grandmother in her seventies, and her uncle, all living in flat 1605 of one building.
He showed screen captures of his WhatsApp conversations with his girlfriend, who had returned home after a school examination. The last message at 3.23pm on Wednesday was: “Fire in the next unit is coming … I am suffering.”
Another user made repeated appeals about a missing 15-month-old baby girl, two relatives, and a domestic helper from flat 1907 of Wang Tai House.

Some families rushed to Prince of Wales Hospital in Sha Tin to try to locate their loved ones. A Post reporter observed at least 20 visitors at the help desk throughout the morning.
Another visitor, 76-year-old Lam, who lost his 80-year-old brother in the fire, was searching for his sister-in-law and nephew, who lived with his brother in the same flat at Wang Cheong House.
Hospital officers told him that there were no admission records for them.
“We don’t know whether we should do [the funeral] for one or for three,” he told the Post.

data.title: Putin says Russia will stop fighting when Ukraine withdraws from territory Moscow claims
data.description:
Russian President Vladimir Putin said on Thursday that Moscow would end its Ukraine offensive if Kyiv withdrew from territory Moscow claims at its own – otherwise they would take it by force.
The Russian army has been slowly but steadily grinding through eastern Ukraine in costly battles against outnumbered and outgunned Ukrainian forces.
Washington has meanwhile renewed its push to end the nearly four-year war, putting forward a surprise plan that it hopes to finalise through coming talks with Moscow and Kyiv.
“If Ukrainian forces leave the territories they hold, then we will stop combat operations,” Putin said during a visit to Kyrgyzstan. “If they don’t, then we will achieve it by military means.”
Russia controls around one-fifth of Ukraine’s territory. The issue of occupied land, which Kyiv has said it will never cede, has become the key stumbling block in the peace process.
Putin repeated the claim that Russia had encircled the Ukrainian army in Pokrovsk and Myrnograd in Ukraine’s eastern Donetsk region – the most fiercely embattled area and a key target for Moscow’s forces.

“Krasnoarmeysk and Dimitrov are completely surrounded,” he said, using the Russian names for the cities.
Moscow was also advancing in Vovchansk and Siversk, as well as approaching the important logistic hub of Guliaipole, he added.
The Russian offensive “is practically impossible to hold back, so there is little that can be done about it,” Putin said.
Ukraine has denied Pokrovsk and Myrnograd are encircled, insisting its forces continue to hold the enemy along the front line.
Moscow launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, triggering the worst armed conflict in Europe since World War II.
It has killed hundreds of thousands of people and forced millions to flee their homes.

data.title: ‘Hongkongers are capable’: deadliest fire in decades ignites widespread aid efforts
data.description:
People from all walks of life in Hong Kong rallied in support of victims of the city’s deadliest fire in decades, ranging from millions in cash donations by major business groups to grass roots efforts by civilians delivering supplies to those in need.
The fire that erupted in seven blocks in Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po on Wednesday, killing at least 75 people, left thousands of residents homeless and in need of daily supplies.
“What happened on Wednesday night appeared to be such a large-scale event in Hong Kong. Everyone hoped to do something for Hong Kong after [the social unrest] in 2019 and the Covid pandemic,” said Paul Wong Wai-ching, a clinical psychologist and an associate professor from the University of Hong Kong’s department of social work and social administration.
“This is a chance for people to give back to society and unite Hong Kong.”
Among them was a group of volunteers who created websites allowing residents to indicate the location of their trapped family members or their status.
Former district councillor Herman Yiu Kwan-ho, one of those behind the website, told the Post that the idea was to do something.
“We would like to help,” Yiu said, adding that “everyone volunteered to do so”.
He said that volunteers initially started by creating a Google spreadsheet, but later created a website due to the huge traffic, allowing residents to report information there.
The volunteers split the work, with some checking WhatsApp messages and others tidying up information.
Yiu revealed that some of the volunteers even took off work earlier on Wednesday to help, and he himself also took a day off on Thursday to assist.
At least 30 business groups and charitable foundations also offered donations, worth at least HK$700 million (US$90 million), to help victims, with the Hong Kong Jockey Club making the biggest contribution at HK$170 million, including HK$100 million via its emergency relief fund to address the immediate needs of victims.
They included cash handouts to different groups, from HK$50,000 to each injured person in a stable condition to HK$100,000 to each seriously injured and HK$150,000 to the family of each deceased.
All gross income from the race meeting scheduled on Sunday, estimated to be around HK$70 million, would be donated to support the victims.
The foundation under the name of the city’s richest man Li Ka-shing also donated HK$80 million, including a HK$30 million emergency fund to support registered charities, particularly those serving the Tai Po area, in providing immediate relief and longer term aid to people affected by the fire.
The foundation would also earmark HK$50 million for follow-up programmes to support recovery efforts.

Other tycoons also joined in the donation drive, including the Jack Ma Foundation, which pledged to commit HK$30 million to provide support to families affected by the fire and firefighters as well as rescue operations.
Alibaba Group and Ant Group, founded by Ma as well, also announced they would donate HK$20 million and HK$10 million respectively to support the rescue efforts.
Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
On a community level, some residents also rushed to blood donor centres for donations, fearing a surge in demand for treating severely injured patients, with queues appearing outside the facilities.
Bottom-up efforts were also witnessed across Tai Po as ordinary residents flocked to offer supplies such as tissue paper, clothes, diapers, blankets, milk bottles, food and drinks as well as distribution services.
By night, several pupils in uniforms – including one from Carmel Holy Word Secondary School – were seen setting up a booth in Kwong Fuk Estate, offering affected residents, many of them elderly, warm pads and hot water.
At the collection point near the Tai Po Market MTR station, numerous volunteers were also packing supplies nonstop and transporting them to locations such as community centres.
“I seldom come to Tai Po but wanted to lend a hand in this situation,” said Glenn Lau, one of the volunteers.
In the evening, volunteers at the Tai Po Market collection point were also seen splitting into two groups: drivers, who transport supplies using cars, and “infantry”, those who carry items on foot and wait outside for assignments.
An administrator will shout out to those in the queue when supplies are needed for transporting or when help is required for loading vehicles, with requests such as “10 infantry to Kwong Fuk Estate”.
A man with a loudspeaker overseeing the site says the operation is largely passive, as volunteers wait for requests from shelters or other points such as Kwong Fuk Commercial Centre, but they are doing whatever they can because “Hongkongers are really capable”.

data.title: Judo to allow Russian athletes to compete at international events under national flag again
data.description:
Russian athletes are free to compete “under their national flag”, the International Judo Federation (IJF) said on Thursday, lifting the neutral status that had been imposed following the invasion of Ukraine in February 2022.
“The IJF Executive Committee has therefore voted to permit Russian athletes to compete under their national flag once again, with anthem and insignia in place, beginning with the 2025 Abu Dhabi Grand Slam,” the IJF said.
The Russian federation reacted with delight to the “historic decision”, with judo being close to the heart of Russian President Vladimir Putin, who is a black belt in the sport.
“Judo is one of Russia’s favourite sports, around half a million citizens regularly participate in it,” Mikhail Degtyarev, Russia’s Minister of Sport, said in a statement published on Telegram.
“It is important to our country; judo is a presidential sport.”

The head of the national federation was pleased that they had been reinstated.
“We are happy that international judo has become the first to have taken this historic decision,” the president of the Russian judo federation, Sergey Soloveychik, said.
The IJF said having previously welcomed back Belarus, who are widely seen as allies of Russia, it was logical Russia should also return.
“Following recent developments, including the reinstatement of full national representation for Belarusian athletes, the IJF considers it is now appropriate to allow the participation of Russian athletes under equal conditions,” it said.
“Historically, Russia has been a leading nation in world judo, and their full return is expected to enrich competition at all levels.”
Russia has been effectively shut out from international sporting competitions since it invaded Ukraine in February 2022, with major sporting bodies including the International Olympic Committee banning Russian athletes from competing under their own flag.
Judo is not the first sports federation to relax the constraints around the Russians.
The International Boxing Association, who at the time were responsible for boxing at the Olympics and run by a Russian, Umar Kremlev, had allowed boxers from Russia to compete under their national flag and the anthem to be played at competitions they organised.
The IJF said the judokas were not responsible for their government’s actions – though the IOC placed strict conditions on Russians appearing at the Paris Games, barring any athletes who had made supportive statements surrounding the conflict.
“Sport is the last bridge that unites people and nations in very difficult conflict situations and environments,” said the IJF.
“Athletes have no responsibility for the decisions of governments or other national institutions, and it is our duty to protect the sport and our athletes.”
This year’s Abu Dhabi Grand Slam takes place this weekend.

data.title: Pope Leo warns of ‘piecemeal’ third world war on Turkey trip: ‘future of humanity at stake’
data.description:
Pope Leo lamented that the world was seeing an unusual number of bloody conflicts during his first trip outside Italy as Catholic leader on Thursday, and he warned that a third world war was being “fought piecemeal”, with humanity’s future at risk.
In his first speech given overseas since his election in May to lead the 1.4 billion-member Church, Leo, the first US pope, said “ambitions and choices that trample on justice and peace” were destabilising the world.
He told political leaders in Turkey that the world was experiencing “a heightened level of conflict on the global level, fuelled by prevailing strategies of economic and military power”.
Trip marks 1,700th anniversary of Nicene Creed
“We must in no way give in to this,” he pleaded at an event with President Tayyip Erdogan after they held a private meeting. “The future of humanity is at stake.”
Speaking before the pope, Erdogan said that he welcomed the pope’s “astute stance” on the Palestinian issue, and hoped the visit would be beneficial for humanity at a time of tension and uncertainty.
In September, Leo met Israeli President Isaac Herzog at the Vatican and raised the “tragic situation” in Gaza with him.
The first US pope chose mainly Muslim Turkey as his first overseas destination to mark the 1,700th anniversary of a landmark early Church council there that produced the Nicene Creed, still used by most of the world’s Christians today.
Leo, 70, landed in the capital Ankara shortly after midday to begin a crowded three-day itinerary in Turkey before heading on to Lebanon. It will be closely watched as he makes his first speeches overseas and visits sensitive cultural sites.

Papal trips abroad draw global attention
Speaking to journalists aboard the papal flight from Rome, Leo said he wanted to use his first overseas trip to urge peace for the world, and to encourage people of different backgrounds to live together in harmony.
“We hope to … announce, transmit, proclaim how important peace is throughout the world,” the pope said at the beginning of the three-hour flight. “And to invite all people to come together, to search for greater unity, greater harmony.”
Foreign travel has become a major part of the modern papacy, with popes attracting international attention as they lead events with crowds sometimes in the millions, give foreign policy speeches and conduct international diplomacy.
“It’s a very important trip because we do not know much yet about Leo’s geopolitical views, and this is the first big chance for him to make them clear,” said Massimo Faggioli, an Italian academic who follows the Vatican.
Leo to meet Orthodox patriarch
Leo was elected in May by the world’s Catholic cardinals to succeed the late Pope Francis. A relative unknown on the world stage before his election, Leo spent decades as a missionary in Peru and only became a Vatican official in 2023.
Francis had been planning to visit Turkey and Lebanon but was unable to go because of his worsening health.
Francis, who led the global Church for 12 years, often said the conflicts raging across the globe reflected a new “piecemeal” world war and pleaded for the end of wars in Gaza, Ukraine, Iraq, Syria and across Africa, among others.
He will fly on Thursday evening to Istanbul, home to Patriarch Bartholomew, spiritual leader of the world’s 260 million Orthodox Christians.
Orthodox and Catholic Christians split in the East-West Schism of 1054, but have generally sought in recent decades to build closer ties.
Leo and Bartholomew travel on Friday to Iznik, 140km (90 miles) southeast of Istanbul and once called Nicaea, where early churchmen formulated the Nicene Creed, which lays out what remain the core beliefs of most Christians today.
On the flight to Ankara, two journalists presented the American pope with pumpkin pies, a staple of the US Thanksgiving holiday that was also taking place on Thursday.

Peace will be key theme of Lebanese leg
Peace is expected to be a key theme of the pope’s visit to Lebanon, which starts on Sunday.
Lebanon, which has the largest percentage of Christians in the Middle East, has been rocked by the spillover of the Gaza conflict.
Last Sunday, Israel killed the top military official in the Iran-backed Shiite Muslim militant group Hezbollah in an air strike on a southern suburb of the Lebanese capital, Beirut, despite a year-long, US-brokered truce.
Vatican spokesman Matteo Bruni said on Monday that necessary security precautions were being taken to ensure the pope’s safety in Lebanon, but he would not comment on specifics.
Leaders in Lebanon, which hosts 1 million Syrian and Palestinian refugees and is also struggling to recover after years of economic crisis, are worried Israel will dramatically escalate its strikes in the coming months and hope the papal visit might bring global attention to the country.

data.title: Hong Kong fire probe centres on widespread use of flammable material by building firm
data.description:
The investigation into a deadly fire at a Hong Kong housing estate has centred on the use of flammable material in the scaffolding that covered the residential blocks, officials said on Thursday.
Police arrested three men from the maintenance firm responsible for the buildings on suspicion of manslaughter, while the city’s anti-graft agency said it was looking into a possible case of corruption.
Secretary for Security Chris Tang Ping-keung had revealed shortly before 2am that authorities believed the rapid spread of the fire was suspicious, while Director of Fire Services Andy Yeung Yan-kin said the use of styrofoam to seal windows was believed to have hastened its spread.
“We have found that on the relevant building walls, the netting and waterproof tarpaulin material, once burned, showed spreading of the flames faster than they should on regulated materials. This is unusual,” Tang said.
The deadly blaze in Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court claimed at least 65 lives, with 51 found dead at the scene, and left 77 people in hospital as of Thursday evening, while full-scale rescue operations were still under way.

Hours later, police announced the arrest of three men, aged between 52 and 68, on suspicion of manslaughter. The trio included two directors and one engineering consultant of Prestige Construction & Engineering Co, the firm in charge of building maintenance at the estate.
A source said the trio were director and operations manager Ho Kin-yip, 52, director Hau Wa-kin, 54, and 68-year-old Steve Wong Chung-kee, who was the person at the firm responsible for safety and signing off projects.
The Independent Commission Against Corruption said it had also set up a task force to investigate potential corruption related to the fire.
“In view of the immense public interest involved, the ICAC today set up a task force to launch a full investigation into possible corruption in the grand renovation project of Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po,” a spokesman said.
Speculation on the cause of the fire swirled online as the blaze raged, including suggestions that netting used on the bamboo scaffolding covering the buildings had been substandard, and that residents had complained of seeing workers smoking while on the scaffolding.
Police Senior Superintendent Eileen Chung Lai-yee, of the force’s New Territories North regional headquarters, said foam materials had been installed by a construction engineering company and officers believed those involved “were grossly negligent, which led to the incident and caused the fire to spread rapidly, resulting in serious casualties”.
The force also revealed that in the only building in Wang Fuk Court not affected by the blaze, every floor had protective nets, waterproof tarpaulin and plastic sheeting, materials that may have failed to meet fire safety standards.
Styrofoam had been used to seal windows on each floor, which could cause the rapid spread of fire.
On Thursday, officers searched for evidence in various locations across the city, first going to the offices of Prestige Construction & Engineering Co at Laurels Industrial Centre in San Po Kong, where they confiscated documents containing any mention of Wang Fuk Court.
A house in Tin Shui Wai was also searched, with one of the suspects in tow, before officers moved on to a residential complex in Ma On Shan. The Post has learned that they were residences of two suspects.
The Post also visited the firm’s premises in San Po Kong, only to find the shutters closed. There was no doorbell, only a facial recognition access device at the doorstep.
An employee working at the office next door said Prestige would usually be open at this hour, but with not many people coming or going.
A Post reporter has also visited 14 Hing Yin Street, the registered address of Wong Hap-yin, a director and shareholder of Will Power Architects Company in To Kwa Wan, Kowloon. However, the address was found to be inaccurate.
The company, which prepared the report that assessed bidders for the project and gave its highest B rating to Prestige, issued a statement expressing deep sorrow over the casualties and property loss resulting from the blaze.
It said it was fully cooperating with police, the Fire Services Department and the Housing Department’s Independent Checking Unit by providing building plans and professional data.
Will Power pledged to help with the estate’s restoration, but declined to comment further at this stage to preserve the investigation’s integrity.
Additional reporting by Lam Ka-sing

data.title: What happens if the Philippine president and vice-president both resign
data.description:
Mounting corruption scandals and an impeachment push have prompted calls for the Philippine president and vice-president to resign, with legal experts saying a little-known constitutional clause provides for a snap election to be called if the roles are vacated at the same time.
A growing chorus from civil society groups and politicians is urging President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr and Vice-President Sara Duterte-Carpio to step down, with Marcos accused by a political veteran of playing a leading role in the multibillion-peso flood-control corruption scandal and Duterte-Carpio continuing to face impeachment calls for purportedly misusing confidential funds and threatening senior officials.
Bayan, or Bagong Alyansang Makabayan, which has long criticised corruption and political dynasties in the Philippines, and other left-leaning groups have been vocal in calling for the two leaders to resign. Bayan chair Teodoro Casino accused the two leaders and others of being “involved in large-scale and rampant corruption” in a social media post on November 17.
Earlier this month, Zaldy Co – a former senior member of the House of Representatives and once a close political ally of Marcos – accused the president of being “the mastermind” of the flood-control scams.
Sergio Ortiz-Luis, president of the Employers Confederation of the Philippines (ECOP), called on Marcos to address Co’s accusation instead of dismissing it as fake news in a radio interview last week.
Urging the president to deal with the country’s “largest corruption scandal” ever, Ortiz-Luis said: “From the very start … we know what the people want – they would like to see the mastermind [punished].”
In the case of Duterte-Carpio, she was impeached in February by the House of Representatives for allegedly misusing hundreds of millions of pesos in confidential funds and issuing death threats against the president, his wife and the speaker of the House. Her trial did not take place after most senators voted to adjourn deliberations on the impeachment.

Supporters of Duterte-Carpio have flooded social media with posts urging Marcos to resign over Co’s accusations and claims by the president’s sister, Senator Imee Marcos, that her brother was a cocaine addict.
On Wednesday, Duterte-Carpio posted on social media to say that she was ready to become president if needed. “I presented myself to you when I was a candidate for vice-president with the understanding that I am the first in line in succession.”
However, senior Catholic leaders have dismissed calls for the president to step down.
“Our advocacy is clear. The Trillion Peso March [this Sunday] is not to bring down the regime,” Bishop Jose Bagaforo, president of Caritas Philippines, said in a statement, referring to the planned rally in Manila against corruption linked to flood-control projects.
Bagaforo, who was speaking on behalf of the church policymaking body, the Catholic Bishops’ Conference of the Philippines, added: “We don’t support any forces calling for a military junta, revolutionary government or similar actions. In other words, we adhere to the call of the Constitution that we should follow democratic processes.”
Percival Cendana, a congressman with the Akbayan party-list group, has warned against a change in leadership in the event of the president’s resignation.
“Their vice-president will be the president. So let us be careful of such groups whose political calls are self-serving, aside from hijacking the anger of the people.”


Snap election scenario
If the country’s two top leaders were to resign, a snap election could be called for their offices in line with the 1987 Philippine Constitution, according to legal experts.
Barry Gutierrez, a former congressman and a law professor at the University of the Philippines College of Law, told This Week in Asia that the concept of a snap election was “embedded” by the framers of the Constitution.
Such an election was held only once in the nation’s history by the late dictator and the president’s father, Ferdinand Marcos Snr, to arrest his waning popularity amid a shrinking economy, massive corruption and street protests following the 1983 assassination of opposition leader and senator Benigno Aquino Jnr.
In February 1986, Marcos Snr proclaimed himself the winner of the snap election that was marred by violence. His then defence minister, Juan Ponce Enrile, and top military officers, however, withdrew support from Marcos Snr, who was later deposed from power in the “People Power” revolution in the same month.
Gutierrez explained that the Constitution had provisions to guide the country if there was a power vacuum under two scenarios.
If the presidency becomes vacant, the vice-president automatically takes over the role.
In the second scenario, “in case of death, permanent disability, removal from office, or resignation of both the president and vice-president,” the Senate president or – if he is unable – the House speaker “shall then act as president until the president or vice-president shall have been elected and qualified”.

As such, the Senate president or the House speaker would only “act as president” until the election for the two highest political posts is over, according to Gutierrez.
Congress has to convene within three days of the president’s and vice-president’s offices becoming vacant and unveil measures within seven days to hold the snap election, which has to be held within 45 to 60 days.
Antonio Carpio, retired Supreme Court senior associate justice, also confirmed to This Week in Asia the specific timelines for such a snap election to be held in accordance with the Constitution.
Gutierrez said, however, that the scenario of Marcos resigning was unlikely to happen.
The president has “bought time” for himself after the Cabinet reshuffle in May, according to Gutierrez. If his administration were to file corruption charges against the individuals behind the flood-control scandal before Christmas, Marcos would still be the leader next year, he added.

data.title: Tai Po footballers pay tribute to Hong Kong fire victims during match in Sydney
data.description:
Tai Po footballers paid tribute to the victims of the fire that has devastated their community as their match against Macarthur went ahead in Australia on Thursday.
Players were visibly moved during a minute’s silence held for the tragedy, which as of Thursday evening had claimed at least 65 lives.
Black armbands were worn by last season’s Hong Kong Premier League winners as they contested the AFC Champions League Two match in Sydney 24 hours after the deadly blaze broke out at Wang Fuk Court.
Tai Po goalkeeper Tse Ka-wing, defender Gabriel Cividini and forward Michel Renner were emotional during the pre-match silence, while some travelling supporters were moved to tears.
The visiting side somehow gathered themselves to deliver a committed performance in a 2-1 defeat.
Igor Sartori opted against celebrating after he gave Tai Po a 24th-minute lead, instead clasping his hands together in prayer.

Luke Vickery equalised for Macarthur when he beat Tse from distance five minutes after half-time, and went on to score the Australians’ second 20 minutes later, converting the rebound after Luke Brattan’s free-kick struck a post.
An impeccable minute’s silence was also observed at Mong Kok Stadium, where Eastern were beaten 5-0 by Gamba Osaka.
Already eliminated, Eastern were brushed aside by the Japanese side, whose fans accounted for around half of the 1,881 crowd.
Ryoya Yamashita slammed high past Liu Fu-yuen after four minutes, then hit a deep cross for the sliding Shota Fukuoka to force home 15 minutes later.
When Dani Almazan missed Keisuke Kurokawa’s 33rd-minute pullback, allowing Shuto Abe to add a third goal, Eastern would have feared a repeat of their 7-0 home defeat by Ratchaburi three weeks ago.
The Japanese team added two more goals after the break. Gaku Nawata flashed a strike inside Liu’s left post, after Deniz Hummett had cushioned a Riku Handa delivery into an unguarded net.

data.title: China has minimal nuclear arsenal, seeks no arms race with US: white paper
data.description:
China will not seek a nuclear arms race and opposes the United States’ deployment of offensive missiles in Asia, according to Beijing’s latest white paper on nuclear policy.
The paper – titled “China’s Arms Control, Disarmament and Non-Proliferation in the New Era” – updates the previous 2005 version of the document.
In the white paper released on Thursday, Beijing said it had consistently kept its nuclear forces at the minimum level required for national security.
Beijing has long underscored its “no first-use” nuclear policy, and has pledged to unconditionally refrain from using nuclear weapons against non-nuclear states or in nuclear-free zones.


While not naming the US, the white paper said “some countries” had been promoting deterrence in the Asia-Pacific region and were adjusting their nuclear policy, which threatened global security.
“China has long exercised extreme restraint in the scale and development of its nuclear arsenal, never competing with other countries in terms of investment, numbers or size,” it said.
“[China] will not engage in any nuclear arms race with any country in the future.”
China has never made public the number of nuclear warheads it has.
However, in proposals for the 15th five-year plan unveiled last month, it pledged to “strengthen strategic deterrence capabilities [and] safeguard global strategic balance and stability”.
Strategic deterrence was seen as a reference to nuclear forces and the move was interpreted as a sign that China expects to narrow the nuclear arsenal gap with the US and Russia.
It echoed Chinese President Xi Jinping’s reference in his Communist Party congress report in 2022 that China would “build a strong strategic deterrence system”.
According to a report published by the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute in June, China has around 600 nuclear warheads, adding around 100 new warheads a year since 2023.
The institute estimates the US has a total inventory of 5,177 warheads, of which 3,700 are in military stockpiles, while Russia has 5,459, of which 4,309 are in military stockpiles.
US President Donald Trump has repeatedly called on China to join the US and Russia in nuclear disarmament talks, an idea Beijing has rejected, citing the disparity in nuclear forces with the US.
“Any actions that threaten or harm China’s core interests will be met with firm countermeasures by China,” the white paper said.
It also criticised the US’ “Golden Dome” system as a move to pursue “absolute security”, adding that it would “pose a serious threat to outer space security”.
Trump declared in January that he intended the US to host the futuristic Golden Dome missile defence system to counter aerial threats, including from ballistic and cruise missiles.
In May, he announced he had selected a design and the system would be operational by the end of his term.


The US has worked to strengthen its deterrence in the first island chain in recent years. It showcased its Typhon intermediate-range missile system in Japan in September before removing it this month. It also deployed the missile system to the Philippines in April last year.
Beijing’s white paper said China had developed missiles and missile defence systems to protect itself.
“Given its vast territory and complex security environment, these capabilities are meant to safeguard sovereignty, maintain security and deter war, and are not directed at any other country or region,” it said.
On Thursday, the State Council’s Information Office said the white paper was intended to show China’s “firm determination and sense of responsibility to maintain strategic stability and reduce the harms of war”.
